title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Psychology of Procrastination
content.subtitle: Why putting things off is rarely about laziness—and what it really reveals about the human mind.
content.text: Despite its universal reputation as a character flaw, procrastination is not, at its core, a problem of time management. It is, according to a growing body of psychological research, primarily an emotional regulation strategy. When we face a task that provokes feelings of anxiety, self-doubt, or boredom, the brain's instinct is to seek immediate relief by diverting attention elsewhere. [31] The writer suggests that procrastination is not a failure of discipline but a misguided attempt to protect the self from discomfort in the short term. [/31] The task itself does not disappear; the relief does not last; and the delay only adds guilt to the original anxiety, creating a self-reinforcing spiral. || What makes procrastination particularly insidious is the way it operates under the cover of plausibility. The procrastinator rarely admits to avoiding the task; instead, the mind constructs a compelling narrative of rational justification. [32] 'I work better under pressure' or 'I'll be more inspired tomorrow' are not honest assessments of one's working style; they are sophisticated forms of self-deception that allow the individual to avoid confronting the true source of their avoidance. [/32] Recognising these internal narratives for what they are is the first and most difficult step toward breaking the cycle. || [33] The author's view on the relationship between perfectionism and procrastination is that they are deeply and paradoxically linked. [/33] The individual who never submits the report because it is 'not quite finished' is not being diligent; they are avoiding the vulnerability of being judged. The perfectionist sets an impossible standard precisely because failure to meet it provides a permanent excuse for inaction. By understanding that 'good enough' is a mark of courage rather than of mediocrity, the perfectionist procrastinator can begin to loosen the paralysing grip of their own standards. || Neuroscience offers a complementary perspective. The part of the brain most responsible for procrastination is the limbic system, which governs our instinctual, pleasure-seeking behaviour. It frequently overrides the prefrontal cortex, the seat of rational planning and long-term thinking. [34] This neurological tug-of-war explains why even people with high intelligence and clear goals can find themselves watching hours of television rather than starting a single paragraph of work they genuinely want to complete. [/34] The problem is not one of wanting; it is one of the emotional brain simply outmuscling the rational one. || [35] Importantly, some researchers draw a distinction between 'passive' procrastination and 'active' procrastination. [/35] The passive procrastinator is paralysed by indecision and delayed by genuine fear. The active procrastinator, by contrast, deliberately delays in order to work under the focused intensity of a deadline, and typically achieves outcomes as positive as those of non-procrastinators. This distinction matters because it resists the simplistic narrative that all delay is destructive. For the active procrastinator, strategic timing is not an avoidance mechanism; it is a performance tool. || The path forward does not lie in sheer willpower, which research consistently shows to be a limited and unreliable resource. [36] The writer concludes that the most effective antidote to procrastination is designing an environment—both physical and psychological—that makes beginning small, manageable, and emotionally safe. [/36] By shrinking the first step to something almost laughably easy, the procrastinator can bypass the emotional alarm that typically blocks action. The hardest part is always the beginning; and the beginning, it turns out, needs only to be tiny.

questions.question: According to the first paragraph, what does the author say procrastination fundamentally is?
options: A) An attempt to avoid unpleasant feelings in the moment.
B) A useful strategy that improves performance over time.
C) A rational method for managing large amounts of work.
D) A sign of poor intelligence and weak character.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the description of procrastination as 'a misguided attempt to protect the self from discomfort in the short term.' A describes a rationalisation, not the author's definition. C and D are not the author's view.

questions.question: In the second paragraph, the author describes phrases like 'I work better under pressure' as...
options: A) evidence that procrastinators have poor self-awareness.
B) subtle forms of dishonesty used to avoid facing the real problem.
C) harmless habits that do not affect a person's productivity.
D) accurate and useful insights into individual working styles.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the description of these phrases as 'sophisticated forms of self-deception.' A is the opposite of the author's view. B is partially implied but not stated directly. D contradicts the author's argument.

questions.question: How does the author explain the link between perfectionism and procrastination?
options: A) Impossible standards are set consciously to justify never starting.
B) Procrastination helps perfectionists produce higher quality work.
C) Perfectionists are never procrastinators because they are too motivated.
D) Perfectionism is a sign of admirable diligence and hard work.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33] and the idea that the perfectionist 'sets an impossible standard precisely because failure to meet it provides a permanent excuse for inaction.' A and C are explicitly contradicted. D is not mentioned.

questions.question: What does the neuroscientific perspective described in the fourth paragraph suggest?
options: A) Intelligent people are immune to procrastination due to stronger prefrontal cortices.
B) The limbic system can be permanently retrained through simple exercises.
C) Procrastination can happen even when a person genuinely wants to work.
D) Watching television is a scientifically proven way to boost creativity.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the image of someone 'watching hours of television rather than starting a single paragraph of work they genuinely want to complete.' A is directly contradicted. B and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What is the main point the author makes about 'active' procrastination?
options: A) It is a form of deliberate time management that can produce good results.
B) It is more harmful than passive procrastination in every situation.
C) It is a myth invented by researchers to excuse lazy behaviour.
D) It is identical to passive procrastination in its causes and effects.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the description of the active procrastinator as someone who uses delay deliberately and 'achieves outcomes as positive as those of non-procrastinators.' A is the opposite. C and D are not suggested.

questions.question: What solution does the writer recommend for overcoming procrastination?
options: A) Developing greater willpower through regular mental discipline exercises.
B) Setting large, ambitious goals to provide powerful motivation.
C) Creating conditions that make the first step feel small and unthreatening.
D) Removing all distractions from the workplace to force concentration.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the advice to design an environment that makes 'beginning small, manageable, and emotionally safe.' A is explicitly rejected as unreliable. B and D are not suggested as the primary solution.
